pattern: Since a single +1 or -1 is the cheapest possible way to flip an element's parity, the minimum operation count is just the number of positions that disagree with whichever of the two alternating parity templates (even-first or odd-first) needs fewer fixes; once that's fixed, minimizing the resulting max-min becomes a 'choose +1 or -1 for a subset of elements' range-shrinking problem, solvable by sorting just that subset and sweeping a raise/lower split point.
hints: There are only two possible target parity patterns for the whole array (starting with even at index 0, or starting with odd at index 0). For each pattern, every index either already matches (free) or needs exactly one +1/-1 to flip its parity (cost 1) — the answer to part one is the smaller of the two patterns' total flip counts.

Once you know which pattern(s) achieve that minimum flip count, the elements that must flip can each independently become (value + 1) or (value - 1) — your choice — while every non-flipped element is locked at its original value. This is now a pure 'minimize max - min' problem over a mix of fixed values and +-1-adjustable values.

To minimize the range with a subset of elements each allowed +1 or -1: sort just the adjustable values, and sweep a split point over them — for a given split, every adjustable value up to the split takes +1 (raising the smaller ones) and every value from the split onward takes -1 (lowering the larger ones); combine with the fixed values' own min/max and take the best split. If both target patterns tie for the minimum flip count, compute this for both and keep the smaller resulting range.

edge_inputs.description: single element — already parity alternating trivially, zero operations and zero range
already perfectly parity alternating — zero operations needed, range is just the existing spread
all-odd negative values — a naive parity check using '% 2' directly (without normalizing the sign) misclassifies negative odd numbers in some languages; this forces every adjacent pair to need a fix
both target patterns (even-first and odd-first) require the SAME minimum number of flips, but they lead to different achievable minimum ranges (5 vs 6) — the smaller one must be chosen

four identical values force exactly two of them to flip parity; the two flipped elements' directions must be chosen to keep the range as small as possible rather than trivially picking the same direction for both
the two extreme constraint values, both already the same parity — exactly one operation is forced, and the resulting range is dictated by the fixed extreme values

stress.description: random arrays of varying length and a wide value range (including negatives), seeded, covering many parity mixes and tie scenarios
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, size)
    nums = [rng.randint(-1000000000, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]
